title: Water Intake & Hydration Guidelines
source: NIH, IOM (National Academies), ACSM, Mayo Clinic

chunks.title: Adequate Intake (AI) of Water by Age and Sex
chunks.content:
IOM/National Academies Adequate Intake for total water (beverages + food, ~20% from food):
Children 4-8 years: 1.7 L/day total (1.2 L from beverages, ~5 cups)
Boys 9-13: 2.4 L/day total (1.8 L beverages, ~8 cups)
Girls 9-13: 2.1 L/day total (1.6 L beverages, ~7 cups)
Boys 14-18: 3.3 L/day total (2.6 L beverages, ~11 cups)
Girls 14-18: 2.3 L/day total (1.8 L beverages, ~8 cups)
Men 19+: 3.7 L/day total (3.0 L beverages, ~13 cups)
Women 19+: 2.7 L/day total (2.2 L beverages, ~9 cups)
Pregnant women: 3.0 L/day total (2.3 L beverages, ~10 cups)
Breastfeeding women: 3.8 L/day total (3.1 L beverages, ~13 cups)
Source: IOM Dietary Reference Intakes for Water (2004), NIH.

chunks.title: Water Intake Based on Body Weight
chunks.content:
General rule of thumb: 30-35 ml of water per kg of body weight per day.
Examples:
50 kg person → 1500-1750 ml/day
60 kg person → 1800-2100 ml/day
70 kg person → 2100-2450 ml/day
80 kg person → 2400-2800 ml/day
90 kg person → 2700-3150 ml/day
100 kg person → 3000-3500 ml/day
Another common guideline: drink half your body weight in ounces (US). E.g., 150 lbs → 75 oz (~2.2 L). Source: NIH, Mayo Clinic.

chunks.title: Water Intake Adjustment for Exercise and Climate
chunks.content:
ACSM (American College of Sports Medicine) exercise hydration guidelines:
- Before exercise: Drink 500 ml (2 cups) 2-3 hours before.
- During exercise: 150-350 ml every 15-20 minutes.
- After exercise: Drink 450-675 ml for every 0.5 kg of body weight lost during exercise.
- For every 30 minutes of moderate exercise, add 350-500 ml to daily intake.
Climate adjustments:
- Hot/humid weather: Increase by 500-1000 ml/day.
- High altitude (>2500m): Increase by 500 ml/day (faster breathing, drier air).
- Air-conditioned/heated environments: Increase slightly (dry air). Source: ACSM Position Stand on Fluid Replacement (2007).

chunks.title: Optimal Water Drinking Timing Throughout the Day
chunks.content:
Research-suggested optimal water timing:
1. Morning (upon waking): 1-2 glasses — rehydrates after 6-8h sleep fast, kickstarts metabolism.
2. 30 min before meals: 1 glass — aids digestion and may reduce calorie intake by 75 kcal per meal (Virginia Tech study, 2010).
3. Before, during, after exercise: See ACSM guidelines above.
4. Mid-afternoon (2-3 PM): 1-2 glasses — combats afternoon fatigue/dehydration slump.
5. 1 hour before bed: 1 glass — prevents overnight dehydration, but not too close to bedtime to avoid sleep disruption.
Signs of dehydration: Dark yellow urine, fatigue, headache, dizziness, dry mouth/skin. Urine should be pale straw color. Source: NIH MedlinePlus, Mayo Clinic.

chunks.title: Overhydration and Water Intoxication
chunks.content: Drinking too much water too quickly can cause hyponatremia (dangerously low blood sodium). Risk threshold: >1.5 L per hour sustained, or >6 L/day total. Symptoms: nausea, headache, confusion, seizures. Most at risk: endurance athletes, people with kidney disorders. Safe rule: drink to thirst, don't force excessive amounts. The kidney can process ~0.8-1.0 L per hour. Source: NIH, BMJ Case Reports.
